Name: informationtheoreticpruningwithdistortion
Title: Information-Theoretic Pruning with Task-Relevant Distortion
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing model pruning as an information-theoretic problem, we can derive a principled method to allocate bits in a neural network such that the task-relevant distortion is minimized while maintaining a desired compression ratio.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the use of rate-distortion theory to understand and improve model compression (Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2019). These studies have shown that pruning is essential for effective compression and that it can improve population risk by reducing generalization error. However, they have not fully addressed the allocation of bits based on task-relevant distortion metrics, which is crucial for maintaining predictive performance.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic approach to model pruning that minimizes task-relevant distortion while achieving a desired compression ratio. By framing the pruning problem as a rate-distortion problem, we derive a method to allocate bits in a neural network such that the distortion in the task-relevant information is minimized. This approach ensures that the compressed model maintains high predictive performance. We validate our method on image classification tasks, demonstrating that it outperforms existing pruning techniques in terms of both compression ratio and task performance.

Experiments.Description: Derive the rate-distortion function for a neural network pruning problem, using task-relevant distortion metrics such as classification error or cross-entropy loss.
Experiments.Algorithmic Changes: Develop an algorithm that iteratively prunes the network while monitoring the task-relevant distortion. Use mutual information and Fisher information to guide the pruning process, ensuring that the most informative weights are retained.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Evaluate the pruning algorithm using the compression ratio, task performance (e.g., classification accuracy), and the rate-distortion curve.

Experiments.Description: Compare the proposed method with existing pruning techniques (e.g., magnitude-based pruning, Hessian-weighted K-means clustering) on datasets such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
Experiments.Algorithmic Changes: Implement the proposed pruning algorithm and existing techniques on the same networks and datasets. Ensure that the same initial conditions and hyperparameters are used for a fair comparison.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Report the compression ratio, task performance, and the rate-distortion curve for each method. Use statistical tests to determine the significance of the differences in performance.

Experiments.Description: Analyze the impact of different distortion metrics on the pruning process and the final model performance.
Experiments.Algorithmic Changes: Experiment with various distortion metrics (e.g., classification error, cross-entropy loss, mean squared error) and observe how they affect the pruning decisions and the final model performance.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Measure the task performance and the rate-distortion curve for each distortion metric. Identify the most effective metric for maintaining high performance under compression.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The derivation of the rate-distortion function for neural networks may be complex and require simplifying assumptions.
The proposed method may be computationally intensive, especially for large networks and datasets.
The choice of task-relevant distortion metrics may vary across different tasks and datasets, requiring careful selection and validation.
The effectiveness of the method may depend on the architecture of the neural network and the specific pruning algorithm used.
